Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلْمُدَّثِّرُ (١)
O you wrapped up in your mantle! (1)
قُمْ فَأَنذِرْ (٢)
Rise up and warn! (2)
وَرَبَّكَ فَكَبِّرْ (٣)
Magnify your Lord, (3)
وَثِيَابَكَ فَطَهِّرْ (٤)
and purify your clothes, (4)
وَٱلرُّجْزَ فَٱهْجُرْ (٥)
and keep away from all impurity! (5)
وَلَا تَمْنُن تَسْتَكْثِرُ (٦)
Do not grant a favour seeking a greater gain, (6)
وَلِرَبِّكَ فَٱصْبِرْ (٧)
and be patient for the sake of your Lord. (7)

(Commentary)

The Surah contains the command to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to warn (الإنذار) within a context that suggests it was among the commands of the early days of the Prophetic mission (البعثة). It then alludes to the greatness of the Noble Quran and the majesty of its status, followed by a severe threat against those who confront it with denial and accusations of sorcery, and a condemnation of those who turn away from its call.

The Surah is Meccan, among the ancient ones revealed at the beginning of the Prophetic mission and the emergence of the call (الدعوة). It has even been said that it was the first Surah revealed of the Quran, although this is contradicted by the very verses of the Surah, which explicitly state that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) had already been reciting the Quran to the people and that they had rejected it, turned away from it, and accused it of being "sorcery handed down."

For this reason, some have inclined toward the view that what was revealed first were the seven verses at the beginning of the Surah. This would imply that the Surah was not revealed all at once. While this is not far-fetched considering the text of the seven verses, it is countered by the context of the beginning of Surah al-Alaq, which clearly appears to be the first part of the Quran to be revealed.

Others have suggested that the Surah was the first to be revealed to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) when he was commanded to make the call public after having kept it secret for a period at the start of the mission. Thus, it carries the same meaning as His saying: فَاصْدَعْ بِما تُؤْمَرُ وَأَعْرِضْ عَنِ الْمُشْرِكِينَ "So proclaim what you have been commanded, and turn away from the polytheists" (15:94). By this, they sought to reconcile the reports stating it was the first to be revealed, those stating it was revealed after Surah al-Alaq, and those stating that Surah al-Muzzammil and Surah al-Muddaththir were revealed together. However, this view does not exceed the level of mere possibility.

In any case, what is certain is that the Surah is among the earliest Quranic Surahs revealed to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). The seven verses we have cited contain the command to warn and the other qualities necessitated by it, which Allah enjoined upon him.

His saying, the Exalted: يا أَيُّهَا الْمُدَّثِّرُ O you enwrapped one! (74:1). Al-Muddaththir (المدثر), with a doubled dal and tha, is originally al-mutadaththir (المتدثر), the active participle of al-tadaththur (التدثر), meaning to wrap oneself in garments when sleeping.

The meaning is: "O you who are covered in garments for sleep." This is an address to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) while he was in this state. He was addressed by a description derived from his current state as a gesture of intimacy (تأنيسا) and kindness (ملاطفة), similar to His saying: يا أَيُّهَا الْمُزَّمِّلُ "O you enwrapped one!" (73:1).

It has been said that "enwrapping" (التدثر) refers to his (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) being clothed in Prophethood (النبوة), likening it to a garment with which one is adorned and beautified. It has also been said that it refers to his (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) withdrawal and absence from sight; thus, it is an address to him regarding his state in the Cave of Hira. Others have said it refers to rest and leisure, as if it were said to him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله): "O you who are resting and at leisure, the time for rest has ended, and the time for the burdens of obligations (التكاليف) and the guidance of people has arrived."

While these interpretations are not inherently problematic, the first meaning is what most readily comes to mind.

His saying, the Exalted: قُمْ فَأَنْذِرْ Arise and warn! (74:2). It appears that the intent is the command to warn (الإنذار) without regard to who is being warned; thus, the meaning is "perform the act of warning." Some have mentioned that the object of the verb is omitted, and the estimation is "warn your closest kinsfolk," due to its suitability for the beginning of the call, as mentioned in Surah al-Shu'ara.

Others have stated that the omitted object is general, referring to all of humanity, based on His saying: وَما أَرْسَلْناكَ إِلَّا كَافَّةً لِلنَّاسِ "We did not send you except as a [bearer] of good news and warner to all mankind" (34:28).

Giving good tidings (التبشير) was not mentioned alongside warning (الإنذار)—even though they are like inseparable correlates in the completion of the call—because the Surah is among those revealed at the start of the mission, and warning was the predominant requirement at that time.

His saying, the Exalted: وَرَبَّكَ فَكَبِّرْ and magnify your Lord, (74:3). That is, attribute majesty (الكبرياء) and greatness (العظمة) to your Lord in belief and practice, in word and deed. This involves hallowing Him, the Exalted, from having anything equal to Him or surpassing Him; nothing shares with Him, overcomes Him, or obstructs Him. No deficiency affects Him, and no description limits Him.

For this reason, it has been narrated from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه‌السلام) that the meaning of Takbir (التكبير) is: "Allah is greater than can be described." He, the Exalted, is greater than every description we might attribute to Him, even this very description. This is what accords with Islamic monotheism (التوحيد), which surpasses the concept of monotheism found in other heavenly religions.

What we have mentioned is the difference between the two phrases of glorification—"Allah is Greater" (الله أكبر) and "Glory be to Allah" (سبحان الله). "Glory be to Allah" (سبحان الله) is hallowing Him, the Exalted, from every privative attribute (وصف عدمي) based on deficiency, such as death, incapacity, ignorance, and the like. "Allah is Greater" (الله أكبر) is an absolute hallowing of Him, the Exalted, from every description we might attribute to Him, whether privative or positive (وجودي), even from this very description itself. This is because every concept (مفهوم) is limited in itself and does not extend to other concepts, while He, the Exalted, is not encompassed by any limit (حد). Understand this well.

It has also been said that the intent is the command to say the Takbir in prayer (الصلاة).

The expression of Him, the Exalted, as "your Lord" (ربك) is not without an indication that His monotheism (توحيده) was at that time unique to the Prophet.

The author of al-Kashshaf said regarding His saying فَكَبِّرْ: "The fa (ف) entered to convey the meaning of a condition (الشرط), as if it were said: 'Whatever the case may be, do not abandon His magnification.'"

His saying, the Exalted: وَثِيابَكَ فَطَهِّرْ and purify your clothes, (74:4). It has been said that this is a metaphor (كناية) for the rectification of deeds (إصلاح العمل). This is not without merit, for deeds are like garments for the soul (النفس) in relation to its beliefs; the outward is the title of the inward. In their speech, the rectification of deeds is often metaphorically expressed as the purity of garments.

It has also been said that it is a metaphor for the purification of the soul (تزكية النفس) and hallowing it from sins and acts of disobedience.

Others have said it means shortening the garments, as this is further from impurity (النجاسة); if they were long and trailed on the ground, one could not be sure they would remain pure.

It has also been said that it means purifying one's spouses from disbelief and disobedience, based on His saying: هُنَّ لِباسٌ لَكُمْ "They are a garment for you" (2:187).

It has also been said that the speech is to be taken literally, meaning the purification of clothes from impurities for prayer. The most likely view according to this is that His saying وَرَبَّكَ فَكَبِّرْ refers to the Takbir of prayer, and the two verses are aimed at legislating the essence of prayer (أصل الصلاة) concurrently with the command to the call.

The objection that these verses were revealed when there was no prayer at all is not valid. While the legislation of the five daily obligatory prayers as they are today occurred on the Night of Ascension (ليلة المعراج)—being ten units (ركعات) in total, then increased by seven—the essence of prayer existed since the beginning of the mission. This is evidenced by its mention in this Surah and in Surahs al-Alaq and al-Muzzammil, and is supported by narrations.

It has also been said that purifying the clothes means adopting noble character (الأخلاق الحميدة) and virtuous dispositions (الملكات الفاضلة).

There are other opinions regarding the meaning of purifying the clothes which we have refrained from citing, as they can be traced back to some of the aforementioned views. The most preferred of the mentioned views are the first and the fifth.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالرُّجْزَ فَاهْجُرْ and abandon abomination, (74:5). It has been said that al-rujz (الرجز), with either a damma or kasra on the ra, means punishment (العذاب). The intent of abandoning it is to abandon its cause, which is sin and disobedience. Thus, the meaning is: "Abandon sin and disobedience."

It has also been said that al-rujz is a name for every foul and loathsome act or character trait. Thus, the command to abandon it is a command to leave everything that Allah dislikes and does not approve of, absolutely. Or, it is a command to specifically leave base and blameworthy character traits, assuming that "purifying the clothes" refers to leaving sins and acts of disobedience.

It has also been said that al-rujz refers to idols; thus, it is a command to abandon the worship of idols.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلا تَمْنُنْ تَسْتَكْثِرُ and do not confer a favor to seek more, (74:6). What the context of the verses provides and what suits the occasion is that "conferring a favor" (المن) refers to spoiling a good deed by mentioning it to the recipient, as in His saying: لا تُبْطِلُوا صَدَقاتِكُمْ بِالْمَنِّ وَالْأَذى "do not render your charities void by reproaches and injury" (2:264), and His saying: يَمُنُّونَ عَلَيْكَ أَنْ أَسْلَمُوا "They count it as a favor to you that they have embraced Islam" (49:17). The meaning of "seeking more" (الاستكثار) is seeing the thing and counting it as much, not the seeking of abundance.

The meaning is: "Do not count as a favor your compliance with these commands, your undertaking of the warning, your magnification of your Lord, your purification of your clothes, and your abandonment of abomination, while viewing that as much and being impressed by it. For you are but a servant who possesses nothing of yourself except what Allah has given you ownership of and enabled you to do. He is the Owner of what He gave you and the One Powerful over what He enabled you to do. To Him belongs the command, and upon you is compliance."

Others have offered different interpretations of the verse that do not harmonize as well with the context. It has been said the meaning is: "Do not give a gift in order to be given more than it."

It has also been said: "Do not count as a favor what Allah has given you of Prophethood and the Quran toward the people, seeking thereby a great reward."

It has also been said: "Do not count the delivery of the message as a favor upon your community."

It has also been said: "Do not become weak in your work by considering your acts of obedience to be many."

It has also been said: "Do not count your giving as a favor upon the people, viewing it as much."

It has also been said: "If you give a gift, give it for your Lord and be patient until He is the one who rewards you."

It has also been said that it is a prohibition of forbidden usury (الربا), meaning: "Do not give something seeking to be given more than what you gave."

His saying, the Exalted: وَلِرَبِّكَ فَاصْبِرْ and be patient for the sake of your Lord. (74:7). That is, for the sake of your Lord. Patience (الصبر) is absolute, encompassing patience during calamity, patience in obedience, and patience against disobedience. The meaning is: "For the sake of your Lord, be patient with the calamities and harm that afflict you in your undertaking of the warning and your compliance with these commands; be patient in obedience to Allah, and be patient against disobeying Him." This is a comprehensive meaning for the various points mentioned in the exegesis of the verse, such as the view that it is a command for the act itself without regard to its object, or that it is patience with the harm of the polytheists, or patience in performing the obligatory duties, and so on.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Durr al-Manthur: al-Tayalisi, Abd al-Razzaq, Ahmad, Abd ibn Humayd, al-Bukhari, Muslim, al-Tirmidhi, Ibn al-Durays, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Mardawayh, and Ibn al-Anbari in al-Masahif narrated from Yahya ibn Abi Kathir who said: "I asked Abu Salamah ibn Abd al-Rahman about the first part of the Quran to be revealed. He said: يا أَيُّهَا الْمُدَّثِّرُ (O you enwrapped one!). I said: 'They say it is اقْرَأْ بِاسْمِ رَبِّكَ الَّذِي خَلَقَ (Read in the name of your Lord who created)?' Abu Salamah said: 'I asked Jabir ibn Abd Allah about that, and I said to him exactly what you said to me.' Jabir said: 'I only tell you what the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) told us.'"

He said: "جاورت بحراء فلما قضيت جواري نوديت ـ فنظرت عن يميني فلم أر شيئا ـ ونظرت عن شمالي فلم أر شيئا ، ونظرت خلفي فلم أر شيئا ـ فرفعت رأسي فإذا الملك الذي جاءني بحراء ـ جالس على كرسي بين السماء والأرض ـ فجثت منه رعبا فرجعت فقلت : دثروني دثروني ـ فنزلت : يا أَيُّهَا الْمُدَّثِّرُ قُمْ فَأَنْذِرْ ـ إلى قوله ـ وَالرُّجْزَ فَاهْجُرْ" — "I stayed in seclusion at Hira, and when I finished my seclusion, I was called. I looked to my right and saw nothing; I looked to my left and saw nothing; I looked behind me and saw nothing. Then I raised my head, and there was the angel who had come to me at Hira, sitting on a chair between the heaven and the earth. I was overcome with fear of him, so I returned and said: 'Wrap me up! Wrap me up!' Then there was revealed: 'O you enwrapped one! Arise and warn...' up to His saying '...and abandon abomination.'"

I say: This tradition is contradicted by other traditions indicating that Surah Iqra was the first part of the Quran to be revealed. This is supported by the context of Surah Iqra. Furthermore, his saying "there was the angel who had come to me at Hira" suggests that revelation (الوحي) had descended upon him previously.

In the same source, Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Abu Hurayrah: "We said: 'O Messenger of Allah, what should we say when we enter the prayer?' So Allah revealed وَرَبَّكَ فَكَبِّرْ (and magnify your Lord), and the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) commanded us to open the prayer with the Takbir."

I say: There is something questionable in this narration, for Abu Hurayrah was among those who embraced Islam long after the Migration (الهجرة), while the Surah is among those revealed at the beginning of the mission. Where were Abu Hurayrah or the companions on that day?

In al-Khisal, from Amir al-Mu'minin (عليه‌السلام) in the "Hadith of the Four Hundred": "تشمير الثياب طهور لها قال الله تبارك وتعالى : وَثِيابَكَ فَطَهِّرْ يعني فشمر" — "Shortening the garments is a purification for them; Allah, Blessed and Exalted is He, said: 'and purify your clothes,' meaning shorten them."

I say: There are several reports with this meaning narrated in al-Kafi and al-Majma' from Abu Ja'far, Abu Abd Allah, and Abu al-Hasan (عليه‌السلام).

In al-Durr al-Manthur, al-Hakim (who authenticated it) and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Jabir who said: "I heard the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) saying: وَالرُّجْزَ فَاهْجُرْ (and abandon abomination) with a damma on the ra." He said: "They are the idols."

I say: His saying "They are the idols" is from the speech of Jabir or another narrator in the chain.

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَلا تَمْنُنْ تَسْتَكْثِرُ (and do not confer a favor to seek more), and in the narration of Abu al-Jarud, he says: "لا تعط تلتمس أكثر منها" — "Do not give while seeking more than it."

Ayahs

فَإِذَا نُقِرَ فِى ٱلنَّاقُورِ (٨)
When the Trumpet will be sounded, (8)
فَذَٰلِكَ يَوْمَئِذٍ يَوْمٌ عَسِيرٌ (٩)
that will be a day of hardship, (9)
عَلَى ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ غَيْرُ يَسِيرٍ (١٠)
not at all easy for the faithless. (10)
ذَرْنِى وَمَنْ خَلَقْتُ وَحِيدًا (١١)
Leave Me [to deal] with him whom I created alone, (11)
وَجَعَلْتُ لَهُۥ مَالًا مَّمْدُودًا (١٢)
and furnished him with extensive means, (12)
وَبَنِينَ شُهُودًا (١٣)
[gave him] sons to be at his side, (13)
وَمَهَّدتُّ لَهُۥ تَمْهِيدًا (١٤)
and facilitated [all matters] for him. (14)
ثُمَّ يَطْمَعُ أَنْ أَزِيدَ (١٥)
Still he is eager that I should give him more. (15)
كَلَّآ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ لِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا عَنِيدًا (١٦)
No indeed! He is an obstinate opponent of Our signs. (16)
سَأُرْهِقُهُۥ صَعُودًا (١٧)
Soon I will overwhelm him with hardship. (17)
إِنَّهُۥ فَكَّرَ وَقَدَّرَ (١٨)
Indeed he reflected and decided. (18)
فَقُتِلَ كَيْفَ قَدَّرَ (١٩)
Perish he, how he decided! (19)
ثُمَّ قُتِلَ كَيْفَ قَدَّرَ (٢٠)
Again, perish he, how he decided! (20)
ثُمَّ نَظَرَ (٢١)
Then he looked; (21)
ثُمَّ عَبَسَ وَبَسَرَ (٢٢)
then frowned and scowled. (22)
ثُمَّ أَدْبَرَ وَٱسْتَكْبَرَ (٢٣)
Then he walked away disdainfully, (23)
فَقَالَ إِنْ هَـٰذَآ إِلَّا سِحْرٌ يُؤْثَرُ (٢٤)
saying, ‘It is nothing but traditional sorcery. (24)
إِنْ هَـٰذَآ إِلَّا قَوْلُ ٱلْبَشَرِ (٢٥)
It is nothing but the speech of a human.’ (25)
سَأُصْلِيهِ سَقَرَ (٢٦)
Soon I will cast him into Saqar. (26)
وَمَآ أَدْرَىٰكَ مَا سَقَرُ (٢٧)
And what will show you what is Saqar? (27)
لَا تُبْقِى وَلَا تَذَرُ (٢٨)
It neither spares, nor leaves [anything]. (28)
لَوَّاحَةٌ لِّلْبَشَرِ (٢٩)
It burns the skin. (29)
عَلَيْهَا تِسْعَةَ عَشَرَ (٣٠)
There are nineteen [keepers] over it. (30)
وَمَا جَعَلْنَآ أَصْحَـٰبَ ٱلنَّارِ إِلَّا مَلَـٰٓئِكَةً ۙ وَمَا جَعَلْنَا عِدَّتَهُمْ إِلَّا فِتْنَةً لِّلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ لِيَسْتَيْقِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ وَيَزْدَادَ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ إِيمَـٰنًا ۙ وَلَا يَرْتَابَ ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ وَٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ ۙ وَلِيَقُولَ ٱلَّذِينَ فِى قُلُوبِهِم مَّرَضٌ وَٱلْكَـٰفِرُونَ مَاذَآ أَرَادَ ٱللَّهُ بِهَـٰذَا مَثَلًا ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ يُضِلُّ ٱللَّهُ مَن يَشَآءُ وَيَهْدِى مَن يَشَآءُ ۚ وَمَا يَعْلَمُ جُنُودَ رَبِّكَ إِلَّا هُوَ ۚ وَمَا هِىَ إِلَّا ذِكْرَىٰ لِلْبَشَرِ (٣١)
We have assigned only angels as keepers of the Fire, and We have made their number merely a stumbling block for the faithless, and so that those who were given the Book may be reassured, and the faithful may increase in [their] faith, and so that those who were given the Book and the faithful may not be in doubt, and so that the faithless and those in whose hearts is a sickness may say, ‘What did Allah mean by this description?’ Thus does Allah lead astray whomever He wishes and guides whomever He wishes. No one knows the hosts of your Lord except Him, and it is just an admonition for all humans. (31)

(Commentary)

The verses contain a severe threat against those who impugn the Quran, casting it as sorcery (السحر), and those who mock some of the truths contained therein.

His saying, the Exalted: «فَإِذا نُقِرَ فِي النَّاقُورِ» — Then when the Trumpet is sounded (74:8). The word naqr (النقر) means striking, and the nāqūr (الناقور) is that which is struck to produce a sound. Sounding the Trumpet (النقر في الناقور) is like blowing into the Horn (النفخ في الصور), a metaphor for the resurrection of the dead and their being brought forth for the final judgment on the Day of Resurrection. The sentence is a conditional clause whose result is His saying: "That day..." etc.

His saying, the Exalted: «فَذلِكَ يَوْمَئِذٍ يَوْمٌ عَسِيرٌ عَلَى الْكافِرِينَ غَيْرُ يَسِيرٍ» — that day will be a day of hardship, not easy for the faithless (74:9-10). The demonstrative "that" (فَذلِكَ) refers to the time of the sounding of the Trumpet. It is not improbable that "that day" (يومئذ) refers to the day when they return to Allah for reckoning and recompense, or the day when all creation returns to Allah; thus, it serves as the temporal container (ظرفا) for the day the Trumpet is sounded. It is permissible to consider a segment of time as a container for some of its parts—such as a year being made the container for a month, or a month for a day—due to a specific literary consideration (عناية). Alternatively, time may be considered multiple and distinct based on its varying attributes or the events occurring within it, and then, by virtue of one of its attributes, it is made the container for itself by virtue of another attribute.

The meaning is: the time of the sounding of the Trumpet, occurring on the day of the return of all creation to Allah, is a time of hardship for the disbelievers. Or: the time of the sounding of the Trumpet is a time of hardship for the disbelievers on the Day of Return—based on His saying "that day" (يَوْمَئِذٍ) being a qualifier for "that" (فَذلِكَ) or for "day" (يَوْمٌ).

The author of al-Kashshāf said: "If you ask: What governs the accusative case (انتصب) of 'when' (إذا), and how can 'that day' (يومئذ) correctly serve as the temporal container for 'a day of hardship' (يوم عسير)? I say: 'When' is governed by what the result of the condition indicates, for the meaning is: 'When the Trumpet is sounded, the matter shall become difficult for the disbelievers.' What allowed 'that day' to be the container for 'a day of hardship' is that the meaning is: 'That time of sounding is the occurrence of a day of hardship,' because the Day of Resurrection arrives and occurs when the Trumpet is sounded." End quote.

He also said: "It is possible for 'that day' (يومئذ) to be indeclinable (مبنيا) in the nominative position as a substitute (بدلا) for 'that' (ذلك), with 'a day of hardship' (يوم عسير) being the predicate (خبر), as if it were said: 'The day of sounding is a day of hardship.'" End quote.

His saying: «غَيْرُ يَسِيرٍ» — not easy — is another description of the day, emphasizing its difficulty and indicating that it is difficult in every respect, not just in some ways.

His saying, the Exalted: «ذَرْنِي وَمَنْ خَلَقْتُ وَحِيداً» — Leave Me to [deal with] him whom I created alone (74:11). This is a word of threat. It has been widely reported that this verse and those following it, up to the completion of twenty verses, were revealed concerning al-Walīd ibn al-Mughīrah; his story will follow in the upcoming "Discussion on Traditions," Allah willing.

His saying: «وَحِيداً» — alone — is a circumstantial qualifier (حال) for the subject of "I created" (خَلَقْتُ). The sum of the meaning is: "Leave Me to him whom I created while I was alone (وحيداً), with no one sharing with Me in his creation, and then I managed his affairs with the best of management; do not come between Me and him, for I am sufficient to deal with him."

It is also possible that it is a circumstantial qualifier for the object of "Leave Me" (ذَرْنِي). It has also been said that it is a circumstantial qualifier for the omitted object of "I created," which is the pronoun referring back to the relative pronoun (الموصول); the meaning would then be: "Leave Me to him whom I created while he was alone (وحيداً), possessing neither wealth nor sons." It was also suggested that "alone" (وَحِيداً) is in the accusative case by an implied verb "I dispraise" (أذم). The best of these views is the first.

His saying, the Exalted: «وَجَعَلْتُ لَهُ مالاً مَمْدُوداً» — and for whom I made extensive wealth (74:12) — meaning spread out and abundant, or extended through continuous growth (النماء).

His saying, the Exalted: «وَبَنِينَ شُهُوداً» — and sons to be at his side (74:13) — meaning present, such that he sees them and is supported by them. This is coordinated (عطف) with His saying "wealth" (مالاً).

His saying, the Exalted: «وَمَهَّدْتُ لَهُ تَمْهِيداً» — and for whom I smoothed [the way] (74:14). Smoothing (التمهيد) means preparation (التهيئة), and it is used metaphorically for the expansion of wealth and status and the orderly arrangement of affairs.

His saying, the Exalted: «ثُمَّ يَطْمَعُ أَنْ أَزِيدَ كَلَّا إِنَّهُ كانَ لِآياتِنا عَنِيداً» — Then he greedily desires that I should add more. No indeed! He has been an adversary to Our signs (74:15-16) — meaning he then greedily desires that I increase the wealth and sons I have given him and the smoothing I have provided.

His saying: «كَلَّا» — No indeed! — is a rebuff (ردع) to him. His saying: «إِنَّهُ كانَ» — He has been... — etc., is the justification for the rebuff. The "adversary" (العنيد) is the stubborn opponent who boasts of what he possesses. It is said that after the revelation of this verse, al-Walīd continued to suffer loss in his wealth and children until he perished.

His saying, the Exalted: «سَأُرْهِقُهُ صَعُوداً» — I will soon overwhelm him with a hardship (74:17). Overwhelming (الإرهاق) is to cover someone with violence. The "hardship" (الصعود) is a mountain pass whose ascent is difficult. He likened the evil recompense and bitter punishment that will befall him to being forced to climb a rugged, difficult mountain pass.

His saying, the Exalted: «إِنَّهُ فَكَّرَ وَقَدَّرَ فَقُتِلَ كَيْفَ قَدَّرَ ثُمَّ قُتِلَ كَيْفَ قَدَّرَ» — Indeed he reflected and contrived. Death to him! How he contrived! Again, death to him! How he contrived! (74:18-20). Reflection (التفكير) is well-known. Contriving (التقدير) based on reflection is the mental arrangement of meanings and attributes by advancing, delaying, affirming, or negating them to reach a desired goal. This man desired to say something regarding the Quran to invalidate its call and please his stubborn people. So he reflected: should he say it is poetry, or soothsaying, or the ravings of madness, or ancient myths? Then he contrived to say: "It is sorcery (سحر) from the speech of mortals," because it separates a man from his wife, his children, and his kin.

His saying: «فَقُتِلَ كَيْفَ قَدَّرَ» — Death to him! How he contrived! — is an invocation against him, as the context suggests, similar to His saying: «قاتَلَهُمُ اللهُ أَنَّى يُؤْفَكُونَ» — May Allah assail them! Where do they stray? (9:30).

His saying: «ثُمَّ قُتِلَ كَيْفَ قَدَّرَ» — Again, death to him! How he contrived! — is a repetition of the invocation for emphasis.

His saying, the Exalted: «ثُمَّ نَظَرَ ثُمَّ عَبَسَ وَبَسَرَ ثُمَّ أَدْبَرَ وَاسْتَكْبَرَ فَقالَ إِنْ هذا إِلَّا سِحْرٌ يُؤْثَرُ إِنْ هذا إِلَّا قَوْلُ الْبَشَرِ» — Then he looked. Then he frowned and scowled. Then he turned back and acted arrogantly, and said, ‘This is nothing but magic handed down. This is nothing but the word of a mortal.’ (74:21-25). This is a depiction (تمثيل) of his state after reflecting and contriving, and it is among the most subtle and eloquent of depictions.

His saying: «ثُمَّ نَظَرَ» — Then he looked — meaning he looked after reflecting and contriving, the look of one who wishes to pass judgment on a matter he was asked to examine—as the context of the depiction suggests.

His saying: «ثُمَّ عَبَسَ وَبَسَرَ» — Then he frowned and scowled. Frowning (العبوس) is the knitting of the brow. It is said in al-Majma‘: "He frowns (عَبَسَ يَعْبِسُ عُبُوساً) when he contracts his face; frowning, grimacing, and knitting the brow are synonyms, and their opposite is cheerfulness and radiance." He also said: "Scowling (البسور) is the beginning of a look of loathing on the face." End quote. Thus, the meaning is: then he contracted his face and showed loathing on his countenance after he had looked.

His saying: «ثُمَّ أَدْبَرَ وَاسْتَكْبَرَ» — Then he turned back and acted arrogantly. Turning back (الإدبار) from something is to turn away from it, and acting arrogantly (الاستكبار) is to refuse out of pride and insolence. These two—turning back and arrogance—are spiritual states (أحوال روحية). They were arranged in the depiction after looking, frowning, and scowling—which are physical, perceptible states (أحوال صورية محسوسة)—because they became manifest through his saying: "This is nothing but magic..." etc. For this reason, His saying: «فَقالَ إِنْ هذا إِلَّا سِحْرٌ يُؤْثَرُ» was coordinated with the particle fa (ف) rather than thumma (ثُمَّ).

His saying: «فَقالَ إِنْ هذا إِلَّا سِحْرٌ يُؤْثَرُ» — and said, ‘This is nothing but magic handed down’ — meaning he manifested his turning back and arrogance by saying, as a consequence of them: "This—meaning the Quran—is nothing but magic (سحر) that is handed down (يُؤْثَرُ)," meaning it is narrated and learned from sorcerers.

His saying: «إِنْ هذا إِلَّا قَوْلُ الْبَشَرِ» — This is nothing but the word of a mortal — meaning it is not the speech of Allah as Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) claims.

It has been said that this verse is like an emphasis for the preceding verse, even if they differ in meaning, because the objective of both is to negate its being a Quran from the speech of Allah. Due to the unity of purpose, the sentence was not coordinated with a conjunction.

His saying, the Exalted: «سَأُصْلِيهِ سَقَرَ وَما أَدْراكَ ما سَقَرُ لا تُبْقِي وَلا تَذَرُ لَوَّاحَةٌ لِلْبَشَرِ عَلَيْها تِسْعَةَ عَشَرَ» — I will soon make him enter Saqar. And what will show you what is Saqar? It neither spares nor leaves [anything]. It shrivels the human flesh. Over it are nineteen [guardians]. (74:26-30). Meaning: I will make him enter Saqar (سقر), which is one of the names of Hell in the Quran, or one of its levels (دركة). The sentence "I will soon make him enter Saqar" (سَأُصْلِيهِ سَقَرَ) is an explanation (بيان) or a substitute (بدل) for His saying: "I will soon overwhelm him with a hardship" (سَأُرْهِقُهُ صَعُوداً).

His saying: «وَما أَدْراكَ ما سَقَرُ» — And what will show you what is Saqar? — is to magnify its matter and inspire awe.

His saying: «لا تُبْقِي وَلا تَذَرُ» — It neither spares nor leaves [anything]. The implication of the absolute negation is that it leaves nothing of those it reaches without burning it, and it leaves no one cast into it without reaching them—unlike the fire of this world, which might leave some of what is cast into it unburnt, and if it reaches a human, for instance, it reaches his body and physical attributes but does not reach anything of his soul or spiritual attributes. As for Saqar, it leaves no one cast into it without reaching them; the Exalted said: «تَدْعُوا مَنْ أَدْبَرَ وَتَوَلَّى» — It will call him who turned his back and withdrew (70:17). And when it reaches him, it leaves nothing of him, soul or body, without burning it; the Exalted said: «نارُ اللهِ الْمُوقَدَةُ الَّتِي تَطَّلِعُ عَلَى الْأَفْئِدَةِ» — [It is] the fire of Allah, kindled, which overspreads the hearts (104:6-7).

It is possible that the meaning is that it does not leave them alive nor does it let them die, which would be in the sense of His saying, the Exalted: «الَّذِي يَصْلَى النَّارَ الْكُبْرى ثُمَّ لا يَمُوتُ فِيها وَلا يَحْيى» — who will enter the Great Fire, then he will neither die in it, nor live (87:12-13).

It has also been said: the meaning is that it does not spare anything cast into it without destroying it, and when it is destroyed, it does not leave it destroyed but rather it is restored to be punished again.

It has also been said: the meaning is that it spares no flesh and leaves no bone; and other things have been said as well.

His saying, the Exalted: «لَوَّاحَةٌ لِلْبَشَرِ» — It shrivels the human flesh. The word lawwāḥah (اللواحة) comes from talwīḥ (التلويح), meaning to change the color to black; it is also said: to red. al-Bashar (البشر) is the plural of basharah (بشرة), meaning the outer skin.

His saying, the Exalted: «عَلَيْها تِسْعَةَ عَشَرَ» — Over it are nineteen. They are in charge of the punishment of the criminals. It was left vague and not explicitly stated whether they are from the angels or others, except that what is understood from the verses of Resurrection—and explicitly stated in the following verse—is that they are from the angels.

Some have inferred that the thing being counted (مميز) in His saying "nineteen" (تِسْعَةَ عَشَرَ) is "angels" (ملكا), then said: "Do you not see how the Arabs, who were the masters of eloquence, understood it as such? For it is narrated from Ibn Abbas: When 'Over it are nineteen' was revealed, Abu Jahl said to Quraysh: 'May your mothers be bereaved of you! I hear the son of Abi Kabshah [Muhammad] informing you that the keepers of the Fire are nineteen, while you are a great multitude. Is every ten of you unable to seize one man from among them?' Then Abu al-Asad ibn Asīd ibn Kaldah al-Jumahī—who was a man of great strength—said: 'I will suffice you for seventeen, so you suffice me for two.'" End quote. You can see that there is no proof in his words for what he claims. Furthermore, he called one of the keepers a "man" (رجلا), and "man" is never applied to an angel, especially among the polytheists about whom the Exalted said: «وَجَعَلُوا الْمَلائِكَةَ الَّذِينَ هُمْ عِبادُ الرَّحْمنِ إِناثاً» — They have made the angels, who are servants of the All-beneficent, females (43:19).

His saying, the Exalted: «وَما جَعَلْنا أَصْحابَ النَّارِ إِلَّا مَلائِكَةً» — We have not made the keepers of the Fire anything but angels (74:31) — to the end of the verse. The context of the verse bears witness that they spoke regarding what was mentioned in the verse about the number of the keepers of the Fire, so this verse was revealed. This is supported by what has been narrated regarding the occasion of revelation (سبب النزول), which will come to you in the following "Discussion on Traditions."

So His saying: «وَما جَعَلْنا أَصْحابَ النَّارِ إِلَّا مَلائِكَةً» — the "keepers of the Fire" (أَصْحابَ النَّارِ) refers to its guardians who are entrusted with it and in charge of punishing the criminals within it, as indicated by His saying: "Over it are nineteen," and as witnessed by His saying afterward: "And We have not made their number anything but a test..." etc.

The sum of the meaning is: We have made them angels who are capable of what they have been commanded, as He said: «عَلَيْها مَلائِكَةٌ غِلاظٌ شِدادٌ لا يَعْصُونَ اللهَ ما أَمَرَهُمْ وَيَفْعَلُونَ ما يُؤْمَرُونَ» — over it are [assigned] angels, severe and mighty, who do not disobey Allah in whatever He commands them, and carry out what they are commanded (66:6). Thus, they are not humans such that the criminals might hope to resist them or endure them.

His saying: «وَما جَعَلْنا عِدَّتَهُمْ إِلَّا فِتْنَةً لِلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا» — And We have not made their number anything but a test for those who are faithless. A test (الفتنة) is a trial and an examination. They mentioned that the "making" (الجعل) here refers to making in terms of informing (الإخبار), not making in terms of creation (التكوين). Thus, the meaning is: "We did not inform of their number being nineteen except to be a test for those who disbelieve." This is supported by the end of the discourse: "so that those who were given the Book may be reassured..." etc.

His saying: «لِيَسْتَيْقِنَ الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْكِتابَ» — so that those who were given the Book may be reassured. Reassurance (الاستيقان) is the finding of certainty (اليقين) within the soul; meaning, so that the People of the Book may be certain that the Quran revealed to you is the truth, as they find what We have informed regarding the number of the keepers of the Fire consistent with what is mentioned in the Book they possess.

His saying: «وَيَزْدادَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِيماناً» — and those who have faith may increase in faith — meaning, because of the confirmation they find from the People of the Book regarding that.

His saying: «وَلِيَقُولَ الَّذِينَ فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ مَرَضٌ وَالْكافِرُونَ ما ذا أَرادَ اللهُ بِهذا مَثَلاً» — and so that those in whose hearts is a sickness and the faithless may say, ‘What did Allah mean by this description?’ The lām (ل) in "so that they may say" (لِيَقُولَ) is for the consequence (العاقبة), unlike the lām in "so that they may be reassured" (لِيَسْتَيْقِنَ), which is for the final cause (التعليل بالغاية). The difference is that their saying "What did Allah mean by this description?" is a form of belittlement and mockery, which is disbelief (كفر) and cannot be considered a goal of His acts, the Glorified, except accidentally—unlike reassurance, which is part of faith. Perhaps the difference between the two meanings is what necessitated the repetition of the lām in His saying: "and so that they may say" (وَلِيَقُولَ).

They have interpreted "those in whose hearts is a sickness" (الَّذِينَ فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ مَرَضٌ) as those with doubt and denial among the hypocrites (المنافقين), and they interpreted "the faithless" (الْكافِرُونَ) as those who openly manifest disbelief among the polytheists and others.

Their saying: «ما ذا أَرادَ اللهُ بِهذا مَثَلاً» — they intended by it belittlement and mockery, referring by "this" to His saying, the Exalted: "Over it are nineteen." The "description" (المثل) is the attribute (الوصف). The meaning is: "What does He mean by describing the keepers as being nineteen? How can this small number be strong enough to punish the majority of the two heavy species (الثقلين), jinn and humans?"

( An Appendix to the Preceding Discourse on Hypocrisy )

Some have mentioned that His saying, the Exalted: «وَلِيَقُولَ الَّذِينَ فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ مَرَضٌ» — based on the entire Surah being Meccan, and that hypocrisy (النفاق) only emerged in Medina—is an informing of the unseen events (المغيبات) that would occur after the Migration (الهجرة). End quote.

As for the entire Surah being Meccan, this is what is determined through transmission (النقل), and a consensus (إجماع) of the commentators has been claimed regarding it. What has been narrated from Muqātil that His saying: «وَما جَعَلْنا أَصْحابَ النَّارِ إِلَّا مَلائِكَةً» is Medinan has not been established through transmission. Even if it were established, it is a theoretical opinion based on the premise that hypocrisy only emerged in Medina and that the verse informs of it.

As for the notion that hypocrisy only emerged in Medina, some have insisted upon it, arguing that before the Migration, the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and the Muslims did not possess such power, authority, or extensive means that people would fear them or hope for good from them, such that they would protect themselves from them by manifesting faith while concealing disbelief. This is in contrast to their state in Medina after the Migration.

However, this argument is incomplete—as we pointed out in the commentary on Surah al-Munāfiqūn in a discussion on hypocrisy—for the causes of hypocrisy are not restricted to fear and protection or seeking immediate gain. Among its causes are greed, even for a delayed benefit; among them are tribalism and zealotry; among them is the settling of habit; and among them are other factors.

There is no evidence for the absence of all these causes in all those who believed in the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) in Mecca before the Migration. Indeed, it has been narrated regarding some that they believed and then turned back, or believed out of doubt and then became upright.

Furthermore, the Exalted says: «وَمِنَ النَّاسِ مَنْ يَقُولُ آمَنَّا بِاللهِ فَإِذا أُوذِيَ فِي اللهِ جَعَلَ فِتْنَةَ النَّاسِ كَعَذابِ اللهِ وَلَئِنْ جاءَ نَصْرٌ مِنْ رَبِّكَ لَيَقُولُنَّ إِنَّا كُنَّا مَعَكُمْ أَوَلَيْسَ اللهُ بِأَعْلَمَ بِما فِي صُدُورِ الْعالَمِينَ وَلَيَعْلَمَنَّ اللهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَلَيَعْلَمَنَّ الْمُنافِقِينَ» — Among the people are those who say, ‘We believe in Allah,’ but if they are made to suffer for the sake of Allah, they take the people’s persecution as Allah’s punishment. And if there comes a victory from your Lord, they will surely say, ‘We were indeed with you.’ Is not Allah best aware of what is in the breasts of the creatures? Allah will surely know those who have faith, and He will surely know the hypocrites. (29:10-11).

These two verses are in a Meccan Surah, which is Surah al-‘Ankabūt, and they explicitly speak of the existence of hypocrisy within it. Even disregarding the fact that the Surah is Meccan, the inclusion in the verse of the mention of "suffering for the sake of Allah" and "persecution" (الفتنة) is the truest witness that the two verses were revealed in Mecca, for there was no suffering for the sake of Allah or persecution in Medina. The inclusion in the verse of His saying: "And if there comes a victory from your Lord..." etc., does not prove revelation in Medina, for "victory" (النصر) has other instances besides immediate conquest.

The possibility that "persecution" (الفتنة) refers to what occurred in Mecca after the Migration does no harm, for those persecuted in Mecca after the Migration were among those who had believed in the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) before the Migration, even if they were harmed after it.

In a similar vein, His saying, the Exalted: «وَمِنَ النَّاسِ مَنْ يَعْبُدُ اللهَ عَلى حَرْفٍ فَإِنْ أَصابَهُ خَيْرٌ اطْمَأَنَّ بِهِ وَإِنْ أَصابَتْهُ فِتْنَةٌ انْقَلَبَ عَلى وَجْهِهِ» — And among the people are those who worship Allah on the verge: if good befalls him, he is content with it; but if an ordeal strikes him, he turns back on his face (22:11) should be understood, if "ordeal" (الفتنة) refers to punishment, even if the Surah is Medinan.

His saying: «كَذلِكَ يُضِلُّ اللهُ مَنْ يَشاءُ وَيَهْدِي مَنْ يَشاءُ» — Thus does Allah lead astray whomever He wishes and guides whomever He wishes (74:31). The word "thus" (كَذلِكَ) refers to the content of His saying: "And We have not made their number anything but a test..." etc.

His saying: «وَما يَعْلَمُ جُنُودَ رَبِّكَ إِلَّا هُوَ» — And none knows the hosts of your Lord except Him (74:31). The Exalted linked the negated knowledge to the "hosts" (الجنود)—which are the massive multitudes He created as intermediaries to execute His commands—not specifically to their number. Thus, in its absolute sense, it conveys that the knowledge of their reality, the specifics of their creation, their number, the work they perform, and the subtleties of wisdom in all of that is exclusive to Him, the Exalted; no one shares it with Him. Therefore, no one has the right to deem their number small or large, or to impugn anything regarding their attributes while being ignorant of them.

His saying: «وَما هِيَ إِلَّا ذِكْرى لِلْبَشَرِ» — And it is nothing but a reminder for humans (74:31). The pronoun "it" (هِيَ) refers back to what preceded in His saying: "Over it are nineteen." Its feminine form is due to the femininity of the predicate (الخبر). The meaning is that humans have no way to know the hosts of your Lord; rather, We only informed about the keepers of the Fire that their number is nineteen so that it may be a reminder (ذكرى) for them by which they may take heed.

It has been said: the pronoun refers to the "hosts" (الجنود); it has been said: to Saqar; it has been said: to the Surah; and it has been said: to the fire of this world—which is the weakest of opinions.

The verse contains an indication that the Quranic addresses are for all of humanity (البشر).

(Narrative Discussion)

In the Tafsīr al-Qummī, regarding His saying, the Exalted: «فإِذا نُقِرَ فِي النَّاقُورِ» — up to His saying — «وَحِيداً»: It was revealed concerning al-Walīd ibn al-Mughīrah—who was an old, experienced man among the shrewd of the Arabs, and he was among those who mocked the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم).

The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) used to sit in the Ḥijr and recite the Quran. Quraysh gathered around al-Walīd ibn al-Mughīrah and said: "O Abū ‘Abd Shams, what is this that Muhammad says? Is it poetry, or soothsaying, or oratory?" He said: "Let me hear his speech." So he drew near to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and said: "O Muhammad, recite some of your poetry to me." He said: "It is not poetry, but it is the speech of Allah—which He has approved for His angels, His prophets, and His messengers." He said: "Recite some of it to me!"

So the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) recited to him Ḥā Mīm al-Sajdah. When he reached His saying: «فَإِنْ أَعْرَضُوا فَقُلْ أَنْذَرْتُكُمْ صاعِقَةً مِثْلَ صاعِقَةِ عادٍ وَثَمُودَ» — But if they turn away, say, ‘I warn you of a thunderbolt like the thunderbolt of ‘Ad and Thamud’ (41:13), al-Walīd shuddered, and every hair on his head and beard stood up. He went to his house and did not return to Quraysh after that.

They went to Abū Jahl and said: "O Abū al-Ḥakam, Abū ‘Abd Shams has leaned toward the religion of Muhammad; do you not see that he has not returned to us?" So Abū Jahl went to al-Walīd the next morning and said: "O uncle, you have lowered our heads, disgraced us, and made our enemy rejoice at our misfortune; you have leaned toward the religion of Muhammad." He said: "I have not leaned toward his religion, but I heard a difficult speech from which skins shudder." Abū Jahl said to him: "Is it oratory?" He said: "No, for oratory is connected speech, and this is prose—and its parts do not resemble one another." He said: "Is it poetry?" He said: "No; indeed, I have heard the poems of the Arabs—their basīṭ, their madīd, their ramal, and their rajaz—and it is not poetry." He said: "Then what is it?" He said: "Let me reflect upon it."

When the next day came, they said to him: "O Abū ‘Abd Shams, what do you say regarding what we discussed?" He said: "Say: it is magic (سحر), for it takes hold of the hearts of people." So He revealed to His Messenger (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) regarding that: «ذَرْنِي وَمَنْ خَلَقْتُ وَحِيداً» — Leave Me to [deal with] him whom I created alone.

He was called "alone" (وحيداً) because he said to Quraysh: "I will take it upon myself alone to clothe the Kaaba for one year, and you collectively for one year." He possessed much wealth and gardens, and he had ten sons in Mecca, and he had ten slaves, with each slave having a thousand dinars to trade with—and that was the qinṭār in those days. It is said that the qinṭār is a bull's hide filled with gold.

In al-Durr al-Manthūr, al-Ḥākim narrated and authenticated it, and al-Bayhaqī in al-Dalā’il via ‘Ikrimah from Ibn ‘Abbās, that al-Walīd ibn al-Mughīrah came to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), who recited the Quran to him, and it was as if he softened toward it. This reached Abū Jahl, so he came to him and said: "O uncle, your people want to collect wealth for you to give to you, for you went to Muhammad to obtain what he has." He said: "Quraysh already knows that I am among those with the most wealth."

He said: "Then say something about him that will reach your people—that you deny it or that you dislike it." He said: "And what shall I say? By Allah, there is not a man among you more knowledgeable of poetry than I—neither its rajaz, nor its qaṣīd, nor the poems of the jinn. By Allah, what he says does not resemble any of this. And by Allah, his speech that he says has a sweetness, and upon it is a grace; its top is fruitful, and its bottom is overflowing; it surely rises and is not risen above, and it surely crushes what is beneath it."

He said: "Your people will not be pleased with you until you say something about it." He said: "Leave me until I reflect." When he reflected, he said: "It is nothing but magic handed down (سحر يؤثر) that he narrates from someone else." So there was revealed: «ذَرْنِي وَمَنْ خَلَقْتُ وَحِيداً».

In al-Majma‘, al-‘Ayyāshī narrated with his chain from Zurārah, Ḥumrān, and Muhammad ibn Muslim from Abū ‘Abdillāh and Abū Ja‘far (peace be upon them) that the "alone" (الوحيد) is the child of fornication. Zurārah said: It was mentioned to Abū Ja‘far (peace be upon him) regarding one of the sons of Hishām that he said in his sermon: "I am the son of the Alone (ابن الوحيد)." He said: "Woe to him! If he knew what the 'alone' is, he would not boast of it." We said to him: "And what is it?" He said: "One for whom no father is known."

In al-Durr al-Manthūr, Ahmad, Ibn al-Mundhir, al-Tirmidhī, Ibn Abī al-Dunyā in Ṣifat al-Nār, Ibn Jarīr, Ibn Abī Ḥātim, Ibn Ḥibbān, al-Ḥākim (who authenticated it), and al-Bayhaqī in al-Ba‘th narrated from Abū Sa‘īd al-Khudrī from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) who said: "al-Ṣa‘ūd (الصعود) is a mountain in the Fire; the disbeliever will climb it for seventy autumns, then fall, and he will be like that in it forever."

In the Tafsīr al-Qummī, regarding His saying, the Exalted: «ثُمَّ عَبَسَ» — he said: "He frowned his face." «وَبَسَرَ» — he said: "He twisted his mouth."

Ayahs

كَلَّا وَٱلْقَمَرِ (٣٢)
No indeed! By the Moon! (32)
وَٱلَّيْلِ إِذْ أَدْبَرَ (٣٣)
By the night when it recedes! (33)
وَٱلصُّبْحِ إِذَآ أَسْفَرَ (٣٤)
By the dawn when it brightens! (34)
إِنَّهَا لَإِحْدَى ٱلْكُبَرِ (٣٥)
Indeed they are one of the greatest [signs of God] (35)
نَذِيرًا لِّلْبَشَرِ (٣٦)
—a warner to all humans, (36)
لِمَن شَآءَ مِنكُمْ أَن يَتَقَدَّمَ أَوْ يَتَأَخَّرَ (٣٧)
[alike] for those of you who like to advance ahead and those who would remain behind. (37)
كُلُّ نَفْسٍۭ بِمَا كَسَبَتْ رَهِينَةٌ (٣٨)
Every soul is hostage to what it has earned, (38)
إِلَّآ أَصْحَـٰبَ ٱلْيَمِينِ (٣٩)
except the People of the Right Hand. (39)
فِى جَنَّـٰتٍ يَتَسَآءَلُونَ (٤٠)
[They will be] in gardens, questioning (40)
عَنِ ٱلْمُجْرِمِينَ (٤١)
the guilty: (41)
مَا سَلَكَكُمْ فِى سَقَرَ (٤٢)
‘What drew you into Hell?’ (42)
قَالُوا۟ لَمْ نَكُ مِنَ ٱلْمُصَلِّينَ (٤٣)
They will answer, ‘We were not among those who prayed. (43)
وَلَمْ نَكُ نُطْعِمُ ٱلْمِسْكِينَ (٤٤)
Nor did we feed the poor. (44)
وَكُنَّا نَخُوضُ مَعَ ٱلْخَآئِضِينَ (٤٥)
We used to indulge in [profane] gossip along with the gossipers, (45)
وَكُنَّا نُكَذِّبُ بِيَوْمِ ٱلدِّينِ (٤٦)
and we used to deny the Day of Retribution (46)
حَتَّىٰٓ أَتَىٰنَا ٱلْيَقِينُ (٤٧)
until death came to us.’ (47)
فَمَا تَنفَعُهُمْ شَفَـٰعَةُ ٱلشَّـٰفِعِينَ (٤٨)
So the intercession of the intercessors will not avail them. (48)

(Commentary)

In these verses, the Holy Quran is exonerated from the accusations they cast upon it. It is established as one of the greatest divine signs (الآيات الإلهية الكبرى), containing a warning (إنذار) to all humanity. Furthermore, following it leads to the liberation of souls from the mortgage (رهانة) of their deeds, which otherwise drive them toward Hell (سقر).

His saying, the Exalted: كَلَّا — No indeed! (74:32) is a rebuff (ردع) and a rejection of what preceded. It is mentioned in al-Kashshaf: "It is a rejection—after having made it a reminder—that it should ever be a reminder for them, because they do not take heed; or it is a rebuff to those who deny that 'one of the greatest' is a warner." End quote. According to the first view, it is a rejection of what preceded, and according to the second, it is a rebuff of what is to come. There is another perspective that will follow.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالْقَمَرِ وَاللَّيْلِ إِذْ أَدْبَرَ وَالصُّبْحِ إِذا أَسْفَرَ — By the moon, and by the night when it withdraws, and by the dawn when it brightens, (74:32-34) consists of oath after oath. The withdrawal (إدبار) of the night is the opposite of its approach, and the brightening (إسفار) of the dawn is its manifestation and disclosure.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّها لَإِحْدَى الْكُبَرِ — it is indeed one of the greatest [portents], (74:35). Commentators have mentioned that the pronoun refers to Hell (سقر), and "the greatest" (الكبر) is the plural of "the greatest [feminine]" (كبرى).

The meaning of Hell (سقر) being "one of the greatest" is that it is one of the greatest calamities (الدواهي الكبر) to which no other calamity can be compared, just as it is said: "He is one of the men," meaning he has no peer among them. This sentence serves as the response to the oath (جواب القسم).

The meaning is: I swear by such and such that Hell (سقر) is indeed one of the greatest calamities—the greatest of them—as a warning to humanity.

It is not improbable that كَلَّا (No indeed!) is a rebuff to his saying regarding the Quran: إِنْ هذا إِلَّا سِحْرٌ يُؤْثَرُ إِنْ هذا إِلَّا قَوْلُ الْبَشَرِ — ‘This is nothing but magic handed down. This is nothing but the word of man.’ (74:24-25). In this case, the pronoun in إِنَّها (it is indeed) would refer to the Quran, insofar as it consists of signs (آيات), or it may be a case of the noun of inna (اسم إن) matching its predicate (خبر).

The meaning would then be: It is not as he said; I swear by such and such that the Quran—its signs—is indeed one of the greatest divine signs (الآيات الإلهية الكبرى) as a warning to humanity.

It has also been said that the sentence إِنَّها لَإِحْدَى الْكُبَرِ (it is indeed one of the greatest) is a justification (تعليل) for the rebuff, and the oath is parenthetical for emphasis, having no response, or its response is implicit, indicated by كَلَّا (No indeed!).

His saying, the Exalted: نَذِيراً لِلْبَشَرِ — a warner to humans, (74:36). This is a verbal noun (مصدر) meaning "warning" (الإنذار), functioning as a specification (تمييز). It has also been said to be a circumstantial qualifier (حال) derived from the context of His saying: إِنَّها لَإِحْدَى الْكُبَرِ (it is indeed one of the greatest), meaning: it has become great and momentous while being a warning (نذيرا), i.e., warning (منذرة).

Other views have been mentioned which are not worthy of consideration, such as the claim of some that it is an attribute of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and that the verse is connected to the beginning of the Surah, the implied meaning being: "Arise as a warner to humanity and warn." Others claimed it is an attribute of Allah, the Exalted.

His saying, the Exalted: لِمَنْ شاءَ مِنْكُمْ أَنْ يَتَقَدَّمَ أَوْ يَتَأَخَّرَ — for such of you as may wish to go forward or stay behind. (74:37). This is a generalization of the warning. The phrase لِمَنْ شاءَ (for such as may wish) is a substitute (بدل) for "humans" (البشر), and أَنْ يَتَقَدَّمَ (to go forward) etc., is the object of "wish" (شاء). What is meant by "going forward" and "staying behind" is following the truth (الاتباع للحق)—the manifestation of which is faith (الإيمان) and obedience (الطاعة)—and not following it—the manifestation of which is disbelief (الكفر) and disobedience (المعصية).

The meaning is: A warner for those among you who follow the truth and for those who do not; that is, for all of you without exception.

It has also been said that أَنْ يَتَقَدَّمَ (to go forward) is in the nominative case as a subject (مبتدأ) and لِمَنْ شاءَ (for such as may wish) is its predicate (خبر), similar to saying: "For whoever has performed ablution is to pray." The meaning would be: Absolutely, for whoever wishes to go forward or stay behind, let him go forward or stay behind. This is like His saying: فَمَنْ شاءَ فَلْيُؤْمِنْ وَمَنْ شاءَ فَلْيُكْفُرْ — ‘So let anyone who wishes believe, and let anyone who wishes disbelieve.’ (18:29). The meaning of going forward and staying behind is preceding toward good and lagging behind it. End quote.

His saying, the Exalted: كُلُّ نَفْسٍ بِما كَسَبَتْ رَهِينَةٌ — Every soul is a hostage to what it has earned, (74:38). The preposition bi (بـ) means "with," or denotes causality (السببية), or exchange (المقابلة). The word رَهِينَةٌ (hostage) means a pledge (الرهن), as mentioned by al-Zamakhshari. He said in al-Kashshaf: "رَهِينَةٌ is not the feminine form of رَهِينٌ (hostage) found in His saying: كُلُّ امْرِئٍ بِما كَسَبَ رَهِينٌ — ‘Every man is a hostage to what he has earned.’ (52:21) for the sake of the femininity of the soul (النفس). This is because if that were intended, it would have been said رَهِينٌ, since the form fa‘īl (فعيل) in the sense of maf‘ūl (مفعول) is the same for both masculine and feminine. Rather, it is a noun meaning 'pledge' (الرهن), like الشتيمة (insult) meaning الشتم (insulting). It is as if it were said: 'Every soul is a pledge (رهن) for what it has earned.'" End quote.

The subtle point in considering every soul a hostage (رهينة) is that Allah has over it the right of servitude (العبادة) through faith and righteous deeds (الأعمال الصالحة). Thus, it is a hostage, preserved and detained with Allah until it pays its debt and fulfills the right of the Exalted. If it believes and acts righteously, it is ransomed and released; but if it disbelieves, commits crimes, and dies in that state, it remains a permanently detained hostage. This is distinct from it being a hostage (رهين) to its deeds—inseparable from what it has earned of good or evil—as previously mentioned in His saying, the Exalted: كُلُّ امْرِئٍ بِما كَسَبَ رَهِينٌ — ‘Every man is a hostage to what he has earned.’ (52:21).

The verse is in the position of explaining the reason for the generalization derived from His saying: نَذِيراً لِلْبَشَرِ لِمَنْ شاءَ مِنْكُمْ أَنْ يَتَقَدَّمَ أَوْ يَتَأَخَّرَ (a warner to humans, for such of you as may wish to go forward or stay behind). For the fact that the human soul is a hostage to what it has earned necessitates that every soul should be wary of the Fire in which it will be imprisoned if it commits crimes and does not follow the truth.

His saying, the Exalted: إِلَّا أَصْحابَ الْيَمِينِ — except the People of the Right Hand. (74:39). These are those who are given their records in their right hands on the Day of Reckoning. They are the people of true beliefs and righteous deeds (الأعمال الصالحة) among the average believers. Their mention and their designation as "the People of the Right Hand" (أصحاب اليمين) recur in several places in His speech, the Exalted. Accordingly, the exception is connected (متصل).

What is gathered from the combination of the excepted and the exception is the division of souls that possess "earning" (كسب) into: souls that are hostages to what they have earned—which are the souls of the criminals (المجرمين)—and souls that are ransomed from the pledge and released—which are the souls of the People of the Right Hand. As for the Foremost (السابقون), the Near Ones (المقربون)—those whom Allah mentioned in various places of His speech and counted as a third group, as in His saying, the Exalted: وَكُنْتُمْ أَزْواجاً ثَلاثَةً — ‘and you will be three groups:’ (56:7) until He says: وَالسَّابِقُونَ السَّابِقُونَ أُولئِكَ الْمُقَرَّبُونَ — ‘And the Foremost Ones are the foremost ones: they are the ones brought near [to Allah],’ (56:10-11)—these have settled in the station of servitude (العبادة) where they possess neither themselves nor the deeds of themselves. Their souls belong to Allah, as do their deeds; thus, they are neither summoned nor reckoned. The Exalted said: فَإِنَّهُمْ لَمُحْضَرُونَ إِلَّا عِبادَ اللهِ الْمُخْلَصِينَ — ‘they will surely be hauled [into punishment]—[all] except the chosen servants of Allah.’ (37:127-128). Therefore, they are outside this classification entirely.

It has been narrated from some that the People of the Right Hand are the angels; from others, that they are the children of the Muslims; from others, that they are those who were on the right of Adam on the Day of the Covenant (يوم الميثاق); and from others, that they are those for whom the "goodly reward" (الحسنى) has preceded from Allah. These are weak views whose weakness is not hidden.

His saying, the Exalted: فِي جَنَّاتٍ يَتَساءَلُونَ عَنِ الْمُجْرِمِينَ ما سَلَكَكُمْ فِي سَقَرَ — [They will be] in gardens, questioning concerning the guilty: ‘What drew you into Hell?’ (74:40-42). فِي جَنَّاتٍ (In gardens) is the predicate of a deleted subject, and the nunation (تنوين) of "gardens" is for magnification (التعظيم). The implied meaning is: "They are in gardens whose description cannot be grasped." It is also possible for it to be a circumstantial qualifier (حال) for the People of the Right Hand.

His saying: يَتَساءَلُونَ عَنِ الْمُجْرِمِينَ (questioning concerning the guilty) means their group asks about the group of the criminals.

His saying: ما سَلَكَكُمْ فِي سَقَرَ (What drew you into Hell?)—meaning what caused you to enter Hell—is an explanation of their questioning, either as an apposition of one sentence to another or by assuming the word "saying" (قول), i.e., saying: "What drew you into Hell?"

His saying, the Exalted: قالُوا لَمْ نَكُ مِنَ الْمُصَلِّينَ — They will say, ‘We were not among those who prayed. (74:43). The plural pronoun refers to the criminals (المجرمين). What is meant by prayer (الصلاة) is the specific devotional turning (التوجه العبادي) toward Allah, the Glorified; thus, it is not affected by the differences in the quantity or quality of prayer across different true heavenly religions.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَمْ نَكُ نُطْعِمُ الْمِسْكِينَ — Nor did we feed the poor. (74:44). What is meant by feeding the poor (إطعام المسكين) is spending on the destitute of society to sustain them and fulfill their needs. Feeding the poor is an indication of the rights of people (حق الناس) in practice, just as prayer is an indication of the right of Allah (حق الله).

His saying, the Exalted: وَكُنَّا نَخُوضُ مَعَ الْخائِضِينَ — We used to gossip with the gossipers, (74:45). What is meant by "gossiping" (الخوض) is engaging in falsehood (الباطل) in word or deed and delving deep into it.

His saying, the Exalted: وَكُنَّا نُكَذِّبُ بِيَوْمِ الدِّينِ — and we used to deny the Day of Retribution, (74:46), which is the Day of Recompense. These are four characteristics that a criminal is naturally prone to be afflicted with, either all or some of them. Since those answering the question are the collective group of criminals, it is correct to attribute all these traits to the whole group, even if some were afflicted with some and not others.

His saying, the Exalted: حَتَّى أَتانَا الْيَقِينُ — until death came to us.’ (74:47). This is a limit for the denial. Commentators have interpreted "certainty" (اليقين) as death, because it is something about which there is no doubt. Thus, the meaning is: "We used to deny the Day of Recompense in the world until death came to us and worldly life was cut off," meaning: "We denied it as long as life lasted."

It has also been said that it refers to the certainty (اليقين) that arises regarding the truth of the Day of Recompense upon witnessing the signs of the Hereafter and beholding the intermediate realm (الحياة البرزخية) — the state between death and resurrection — at the time of death and after it. This is a fine meaning.

His saying, the Exalted: فَما تَنْفَعُهُمْ شَفاعَةُ الشَّافِعِينَ — So the intercession of the intercessors will not avail them. (74:48). It was previously mentioned in the discussion on intercession (الشفاعة) — the act of mediating on behalf of others before Allah — that this verse contains an indication that there are indeed intercessors who intercede and whose intercession is accepted, but their intercession does not benefit these people because they are deprived of attaining it.

We have cited a number of narrations (أخبار) regarding intercession in the first volume of this book.

Ayahs

فَمَا لَهُمْ عَنِ ٱلتَّذْكِرَةِ مُعْرِضِينَ (٤٩)
What is the matter with them that they evade the Reminder (49)
كَأَنَّهُمْ حُمُرٌ مُّسْتَنفِرَةٌ (٥٠)
as if they were terrified asses (50)
فَرَّتْ مِن قَسْوَرَةٍۭ (٥١)
fleeing from a lion? (51)
بَلْ يُرِيدُ كُلُّ ٱمْرِئٍ مِّنْهُمْ أَن يُؤْتَىٰ صُحُفًا مُّنَشَّرَةً (٥٢)
But everyone of them desires to be given unrolled scriptures [from Allah]! (52)
كَلَّا ۖ بَل لَّا يَخَافُونَ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةَ (٥٣)
No! Indeed, they do not fear the Hereafter. (53)
كَلَّآ إِنَّهُۥ تَذْكِرَةٌ (٥٤)
No! It is indeed a reminder. (54)
فَمَن شَآءَ ذَكَرَهُۥ (٥٥)
So let anyone who wishes be mindful of it. (55)
وَمَا يَذْكُرُونَ إِلَّآ أَن يَشَآءَ ٱللَّهُ ۚ هُوَ أَهْلُ ٱلتَّقْوَىٰ وَأَهْلُ ٱلْمَغْفِرَةِ (٥٦)
And they will not be mindful unless Allah wishes. He is worthy of [your] being wary [of Him] and He is worthy to forgive. (56)

(Commentary)

In the sense of drawing a conclusion from the preceding warnings and promises, the discourse is presented in the form of wonder at their turning away from the reminder of the Quran and their aversion to the plain truth. It is as if it were said: Since the matter is such, it is incumbent upon them to respond to the call of truth and to take heed through the reminder; thus, it is a matter of wonder that they are turning away from it. No indeed! Rather, they do not believe in the Message, and every man among them desires that a book from Allah be sent down specifically to him. No indeed! Rather, they do not fear the Hereafter, and thus they are not deterred by any warning.

Then, He offers the reminder to them as a proposal, such that they have the choice to accept or reject; if they wish, they may accept, and if they wish, they may reject. However, they must know that they are not independent (مستقلين) in their volition, nor can they frustrate Allah, the Glorified. They cannot take heed unless Allah wills, for the decree of destiny (القدر) is absolutely operative concerning them.

His saying, the Exalted: فَما لَهُمْ عَنِ التَّذْكِرَةِ مُعْرِضِينَ "What is the matter with them that they should turn away from the Reminder," (74:49) is a derivation from the preceding reminder and exhortation. The interrogation is for expressing wonder. The phrase لَهُمْ "with them" relates to a suppressed verb, the estimation being: "What has happened to them?" and مُعْرِضِينَ "turning away" is a circumstantial qualifier (حال) for the pronoun in لَهُمْ, while عَنِ التَّذْكِرَةِ "from the Reminder" relates to "turning away."

The meaning is: Since the matter is such, what thing has occurred—or happened—to the polytheists who deny the reminder of the Quran, such that they are in a state of turning away from it? That is, it was obligatory upon them to believe and have faith, yet they turned away from it, which is a cause for wonder.

His saying, the Exalted: كَأَنَّهُمْ حُمُرٌ مُسْتَنْفِرَةٌ فَرَّتْ مِنْ قَسْوَرَةٍ "as if they were startled asses, fleeing from a lion?" (74:50-51). This is a comparison of them in terms of their state of turning away from the reminder. The word حُمُرٌ is the plural of حمار (ass), and what is meant here are wild asses. مُسْتَنْفِرَةٌ (startled) denotes a state of being spooked or in flight. قَسْوَرَةٍ refers to a lion or a hunter; it has been interpreted as both meanings.

The meaning is: they turn away from the reminder as if they were wild asses that have bolted in terror from a lion or a hunter.

His saying, the Exalted: بَلْ يُرِيدُ كُلُّ امْرِئٍ مِنْهُمْ أَنْ يُؤْتى صُحُفاً مُنَشَّرَةً "Indeed, every one of them desires to be given unrolled manuscripts." (74:52). What is meant by "unrolled manuscripts" (صحفاً منشرة) is a heavenly book containing the true call.

The speech contains a transition (إضراب) from the mention of their turning away. The meaning is that their turning away from the reminder is not merely due to aversion; rather, every man among them desires that a book be sent down to him from Allah, containing what the call of the Quran contains.

Attributing this to them is an allusion to their arrogance (استكبار) toward Allah, the Glorified—that they would only accept His call and not reject it if He were to call each one of them by sending down a heavenly book to him independently. As for the call through the medium of Messengership (الرسالة), they will not respond to it, even if it is the truth supported by manifest signs.

Thus, the verse carries the same meaning as what Allah, the Glorified, narrated of their saying: لَنْ نُؤْمِنَ حَتَّى نُؤْتى مِثْلَ ما أُوتِيَ رُسُلُ اللهِ "‘We will not believe until we are given the like of what was given to Allah’s apostles.’" (6:124), and the meaning of what the nations said to their Messengers: إِنْ أَنْتُمْ إِلَّا بَشَرٌ مِثْلُنا "‘You are nothing but humans like us,’" (14:10) based on what we have established regarding their argument for denying the Messengership of the Messengers.

It has been said that the verse carries the meaning of their saying to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم which Allah narrated in His saying: وَلَنْ نُؤْمِنَ لِرُقِيِّكَ حَتَّى تُنَزِّلَ عَلَيْنا كِتاباً نَقْرَؤُهُ "‘and we will not believe your ascension until you bring down to us a book that we may read.’" (17:93).

However, this is refuted by the fact that the implication of the verse under discussion is that unrolled manuscripts be sent down to each one of them individually, distinct from what is sent to others, not the descent of a single book from heaven upon the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم for everyone to read, as is the implication of the verse in Surah al-Isra.

It has also been said: What is meant is the descent of books from heaven to them addressed with their names, commanding them to believe in Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم.

It has further been said: What is meant is that books be sent down to them from heaven granting them immunity from punishment and the completion of favor so that they might believe, otherwise they would remain in their disbelief. Other interpretations have also been mentioned.

All of these are meanings far removed from the context (السياق), and reliance should be placed on what we have previously stated.

His saying, the Exalted: كَلَّا بَلْ لا يَخافُونَ الْآخِرَةَ "No indeed! Rather they do not fear the Hereafter." (74:53). This is a rebuff (ردع) to them regarding their desire for a heavenly book to be sent to each one of them. For the call of Messengership is supported by manifest signs and definitive proofs that leave no room for doubt for any doubter; thus, the proof (الحجة) is complete and established for the Messenger and others alike, without any need for every person among those called to be given unrolled manuscripts.

Furthermore, Messengership requires a purity of essence (طهارة الذات) and a capacity of the soul (صلاحية النفس) that the souls of other people lack, as is the implication of the response of the Exalted in Surah al-An'am to their saying: لَنْ نُؤْمِنَ حَتَّى نُؤْتى مِثْلَ ما أُوتِيَ رُسُلُ اللهِ "‘We will not believe until we are given the like of what was given to Allah’s apostles.’" (6:124) with His saying: اللهُ أَعْلَمُ حَيْثُ يَجْعَلُ رِسالَتَهُ "Allah knows best where to place His apostleship." (6:124).

His saying: بَلْ لا يَخافُونَ الْآخِرَةَ "Rather they do not fear the Hereafter" is a transition from His saying: يُرِيدُ كُلُّ امْرِئٍ مِنْهُمْ "every one of them desires..." etc. The meaning is that their proposal for the descent of a book upon every man among them is a superficial claim by which they intend to deflect the call from themselves. The real reason for their disbelief and denial of the call is that they do not fear the Hereafter. Had they feared it, they would have believed and would not have proposed a sign after the proof had been established by the appearance of manifest signs.

His saying, the Exalted: كَلَّا إِنَّهُ تَذْكِرَةٌ "No indeed! It is a reminder." (74:54). This is a second rebuff to their proposal for the descent of a heavenly book for every man among them. The meaning is: We shall not send down such a book; indeed, the Quran is a reminder (تذكرة) and an exhortation by which We admonish them, and We intend nothing more than that. The consequence of this is the recompense We have prepared for the obedient and the disobedient.

His saying, the Exalted: فَمَنْ شاءَ ذَكَرَهُ "So let anyone who wishes take heed of it." (74:55). That is, whoever wishes may be admonished by it, for it is only a call within the sphere of choice (الاختيار) without any compulsion.

His saying, the Exalted: وَما يَذْكُرُونَ إِلَّا أَنْ يَشاءَ اللهُ هُوَ أَهْلُ التَّقْوى وَأَهْلُ الْمَغْفِرَةِ "But they will not take heed unless Allah wishes. He is the source of godwariness and the source of forgiveness." (74:56). This is a refutation of what they might mistakenly assume from His saying: فَمَنْ شاءَ ذَكَرَهُ "So let anyone who wishes take heed of it"—namely, that the matter is entirely up to them and that they are independent (مستقلون) in their volition and the actions resulting from it. Thus, if they did not wish to take heed and did not do so, they would have overcome the Exalted in what He intended and frustrated Him in what He willed regarding their taking heed.

The summary of this refutation is that the decree of destiny (القدر) applies to their actions just as it applies to all other events. Their taking heed—if they do take heed—even though it is an intentional act (فعل اختياري) proceeding from them by their choice without compulsion, the Divine Will (المشية الإلهية) is nonetheless attached to it insofar as it is intentional. This means that Allah the Exalted wills, with a creative will (إرادة تكوينية), that the human being perform such-and-such an act by his own will and choice. Thus, the act is intentional and possible (ممكن) in relation to the human being, while it is simultaneously the object of the Divine Will and necessarily realized (ضروري التحقق) in relation to it; without it, the act would not occur.

His saying: هُوَ أَهْلُ التَّقْوى وَأَهْلُ الْمَغْفِرَةِ "He is the source of godwariness and the source of forgiveness" means He is worthy of being feared (يتقى منه) because He possesses absolute authority (الولاية المطلقة) over everything, and in His hand lies the happiness and wretchedness of man. He is also worthy of forgiving those who are wary of Him because He is All-Forgiving, Most Merciful.

The sentence—that is, His saying: هُوَ أَهْلُ التَّقْوى وَأَهْلُ الْمَغْفِرَةِ—is suitable as a justification for the preceding call in His saying: إِنَّهُ تَذْكِرَةٌ فَمَنْ شاءَ ذَكَرَهُ "It is a reminder. So let anyone who wishes take heed of it," which is apparent. It is also suitable as a justification for His saying: وَما يَذْكُرُونَ إِلَّا أَنْ يَشاءَ اللهُ "But they will not take heed unless Allah wishes." For His being the source of godwariness (أهل التقوى) and the source of forgiveness (أهل المغفرة) is not realized except by His possessing an effective will (إرادة نافذة) over them that permeates their actions. Thus, they are not left to themselves and their desires such that they could frustrate Allah through their rebellion and arrogance.

(Narrative Discussion)

In Tafsir al-Qummi, in a narration from Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of the Exalted: بَلْ يُرِيدُ كُلُّ امْرِئٍ مِنْهُمْ أَنْ يُؤْتى صُحُفاً مُنَشَّرَةً "Indeed, every one of them desires to be given unrolled manuscripts." He said: "That was because they said: 'O Muhammad, it has reached us that when a man from the Children of Israel committed a sin, he would wake up to find his sin and its expiation written at his headrest.'"

Then Gabriel descended to the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم and said: "Your people are asking you for the custom of the Children of Israel regarding sins. If they wish, We shall do that to them, but We shall seize them as We used to seize the Children of Israel." They claimed that the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم disliked that for his people.

I say: The story does not harmonize with the tone of the verse, and the narration is not devoid of an indication toward the weakness of the story.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, it is narrated by 'Abd ibn Humayd, Ibn Jarir, and Ibn al-Mundhir from al-Suddi from Abu Salih, who said: "They said: 'If Muhammad is truthful, let there appear under the head of every man among us a manuscript containing his acquittal and security from the Fire.' Then it was revealed: بَلْ يُرِيدُ كُلُّ امْرِئٍ مِنْهُمْ أَنْ يُؤْتى صُحُفاً مُنَشَّرَةً 'Indeed, every one of them desires to be given unrolled manuscripts.'"

I say: The context of the verses and the rebuff contained therein do not harmonize with this story.

In the same source, it is narrated by 'Abd ibn Humayd and Ibn al-Mundhir from Mujahid regarding بَلْ يُرِيدُ كُلُّ امْرِئٍ مِنْهُمْ أَنْ يُؤْتى صُحُفاً مُنَشَّرَةً "Indeed, every one of them desires to be given unrolled manuscripts." He said: "[Addressed] to so-and-so, son of so-and-so, from the Lord of the Worlds—a manuscript would be placed at the head of every man when he wakes up, which he would read."

I say: What is in this narration can be reconciled with the previous narration and with the meaning of the verse we have presented.

In the same source, it is narrated by 'Abd ibn Humayd and Ibn al-Mundhir from Qatadah regarding the saying of the Exalted: بَلْ يُرِيدُ كُلُّ امْرِئٍ مِنْهُمْ أَنْ يُؤْتى صُحُفاً مُنَشَّرَةً "Indeed, every one of them desires to be given unrolled manuscripts." He said: "Some people said to Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم: 'If it would please you that we follow you, then bring us a book specifically for us, commanding us to follow you.'"

I say: This narration is applicable to the interpretation of the verse stating that it carries the meaning of His saying: وَلَنْ نُؤْمِنَ لِرُقِيِّكَ "and we will not believe your ascension..." (17:93), and we have already discussed the problems with that.

In Tafsir al-Qummi, regarding the saying of the Exalted: هُوَ أَهْلُ التَّقْوى وَأَهْلُ الْمَغْفِرَةِ "He is the source of godwariness and the source of forgiveness." He said: "He is worthy of being feared and worthy of forgiving."

In al-Tawhid, with its chain of narration to Abu Basir from Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: هُوَ أَهْلُ التَّقْوى وَأَهْلُ الْمَغْفِرَةِ "He is the source of godwariness and the source of forgiveness." He said: "Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, said: 'I am worthy of being feared—that My servant should not associate anything with Me—and I am worthy, if My servant does not associate anything with Me, to admit him into Paradise.'"

And he said: "Indeed, Allah, Blessed and Exalted is He, has sworn by His Might and Majesty that He will not punish the people of His monotheism (أهل توحيده) with the Fire."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, it is narrated by Ibn Mardawayh from 'Abdullah ibn Dinar, who said: "I heard Abu Hurayrah, Ibn 'Umar, and Ibn 'Abbas saying: 'The Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم was asked about the saying of Allah: هُوَ أَهْلُ التَّقْوى وَأَهْلُ الْمَغْفِرَةِ "He is the source of godwariness and the source of forgiveness." He said: "Allah says: I am worthy of being feared—so that no partner is set up with Me. If I am feared and no partner is set up with Me, then I am worthy of forgiving whatever is besides that."'"

I say: There are several narrations from him صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم carrying this same meaning.
